# Importing Required libraries
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import plotly.express as px
add Code add Text
spark Gemini 
# Required Dataset

np.random.seed(0)
n = 200

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'age':      np.random.randint(18, 60, n),
    'income':   np.random.randint(20000, 120000, n),
    'spend':    np.random.randint(500, 8000, n),
    'category': np.random.choice(['Electronics', 'Clothing', 'Groceries', 'Sports'], n)
})

# Task 1: Bar Chart — Average Spend by Category

keyboard_arrow_down
Purpose :


To visualize the average spending per category using Matplotlib and identify the category with the highest average spend.



        

      


      

        
        

          subdirectory_arrow_right
          1 cell hidden
        

      

      

        
          add
          Code
        
        
          add
          Text
        
        
        
      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    

      

        

          
          

            
          

        

      
avg_spend = df.groupby('category')['spend'].mean()
plt.figure()
avg_spend.plot(kind='bar')
plt.title('Average Spend by Category')
plt.xlabel('Category')
plt.ylabel('Average Spend')
plt.show()

### Purpose: To analyze the relationship between income and spending.

Purpose: To analyze the relationship between income and spending.

plt.figure()
sns.scatterplot(x='income', y='spend', data=df )
plt.title('Income vs Spend')
plt.xlabel('Income')
plt.ylabel('Spend')
plt.show()

## Observation
###There is a weak to moderate positive relationship between income and 
spending, indicating that higher income may lead to higher spending, but the 
pattern is not very strong.

        

          
keyboard_arrow_down
Observation


There is a weak to moderate positive relationship between income and spending, indicating that higher income may lead to higher spending, but the pattern is not very strong.

##Observation:
###In an interactive plot, you can hover to view exact values and categories, 
which is not possible in a static plot.

        

          
keyboard_arrow_down
Observation:


In an interactive plot, you can hover to view exact values and categories, which is not possible in a static plot.



        

      


      

        
        

          subdirectory_arrow_right
          2 cells hidden
        

      

      

        
          add
          Code
        
        
          add
          Text
        
        
        
      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    

      

        

          
          

            
          

        

      
import plotly.express as px

fig = px.scatter(df, x="income", y="spend", color="category",
                 title="Income vs Spend by Category")

fig.show()

# SAVE interactive plot
fig.write_html("task3_plot.html")
